title: Adding Portuguese (Portugal) translation
author: RafaelPaulo12
body:
Hi! I'd like to contribute a Portuguese (Portugal) (`pt-PT`) translation for Coral.

As a `pt-PT` speaker, I noticed this locale is not yet available in the `locales/` directory, and I'd like to add it to make Coral more accessible to Portuguese-speaking communities in Portugal.

### What I plan to do

- Add `pt-PT` FTL translation files to `locales/` and `server/src/core/server/locales/`
- Use the `./scripts/i18n/validate.ts pt-PT` script to ensure all keys are covered
- Add `pt-PT` to `common/lib/helpers/i18n/locales.ts` once coverage is sufficient

### Notes

- I'm aware that strings must not be changed after being committed — new strings will use new IDs as per the contribution guidelines
- I'll follow all the conventions described in the Contributing a Translation guide

Please let me know if there's anything specific you'd like me to address or if this is already being worked on. Happy to collaborate!

title: Feature idea: Delete own comments
author: helderdb
body:
## Search Terms

User delete own comments

## Suggestion

As previously described in https://github.com/coralproject/talk/issues/1534, we want a user to be able to delete their own comments.
Currently there's no endpoints to support this.

## Use Cases
A user should be able to delete (and tombstone) their own comments.

This sums it up pretty well in the original issue:
DELETE MY COMMENT | As a commenter, I want to delete my comment so that it doesn't show publicly anymore
```
- Creates a tombstone
- The Delete button is probably in the Edit button area
- Single default tombstone language
- Moderator can see the text of the comment that was removed in the user drawer
- Replies stay visible
```

MOD TOMBSTONE OPTION | As a moderator, I want the ability to tombstone comments I reject so that I can be transparent about my actions.
```
- Dropdown template language reject options instead of the single Reject button
- Option chosen is shown in the comment card in the admin panel
- Replies stay visible
```

HIDE DELETED | As a readeer, I want to hide all deleted comments and their replies so that my reading experience isn’t too cluttered by things I can’t read
```
- Add ‘Hide deleted comments and their replies’ to Viewing Options, like Off Topic
```

CONFIG DELETE | As an org, I want comment deletion to be configurable, so I can decide how this functions
```
- Add to Configure page "Allow commenters to delete their comments" (site-wide)
- Add to Stream configure "Allow commenters to delete their comments" (stream-only)
- Our default for these would be 'off'
```

Additionally, I would also add the time someone can delete their own comment just like the edit timeframe

title: CommentCache.sortWithRedis can seriously degrade performance and seems unnecessary
author: fdintino
body:
The code that [sorts comments using redis keys](https://github.com/coralproject/talk/blob/da5c0398a252339f5b727eaa795d65f9730e4482/server/src/core/server/data/cache/commentCache.ts#L491-L532) can result in what seems like an excessive number of redis calls. On a recent `AllCommentsTabQuery` for a stream with 900 comments there were a total of 1,815 ZRANGE, ORGANIZE, and EXISTS calls made.

What's more, I'm not quite clear on what value the redis sort logic provides. If the sort cache key doesn't exist it falls back to a straightforward array sort ([`CommentCache.sortComments`](https://github.com/coralproject/talk/blob/da5c0398a252339f5b727eaa795d65f9730e4482/server/src/core/server/data/cache/commentCache.ts#L875-L898)) using data that has already been queried (so it's not as though the redis sort is taking the place of potentially slower mongo queries).

I've opened this ticket to get confirmation that I'm not overlooking something with the cache-based comment sorting. If it turns out to be unnecessary I'd be happy to open a pull request removing this code.

title: Ability to attach files to comment posts

title: Standalone /my-profile page for comment history and replies (outside embed)
author: SamHobbsOrg
body:
## Description

I am a programmer but this is posted in the role of a Los Angeles Times subscriber. The LA Times uses Coral Talk.

This has been generated by Perplexity AI and I have modified the suggestion.

Add a dedicated, bookmarkable page (e.g., `/my-profile` or `/my-comments`) where logged-in users can directly view their comment history, reactions, and replies without the need to load an article's comment embed first.

### Use Case
- Users on sites like LA Times (which uses Coral Talk) currently must open an article's comments, then click the "My Profile" tab to see replies/notifications.
- A standalone page would allow direct access via a URL, improving UX for checking notifications (like on mobile or via bookmark).

### Current Behavior
"My Profile" only exists as a tab inside the comment stream widget (per docs: https://legacy.docs.coralproject.net/talk/commenter-features/). No direct route exposed.

What happens is that I often do not see a reply until after the time has expired for me to reply. It would be better if I had a separate page I could open every day to see replies.

### Proposed Solution
- New route `/my-profile` that loads the user's own comments stream (via existing GraphQL?).
- Reuse the existing "My Profile" tab content: history, reactions, replies, email settings, ignored users.
- Optional: Site admins could customize the path or disable it.

### Benefits
- Better notification handling (e.g., email deep-links to this page).
- Reduces friction for active commenters.
- Aligns with modern apps (e.g., Reddit's profile pages).
- Enables replies to replies in a timely manner.

I will be happy to provide further implementation details if possible.

title: Authenticated GraphQL responses may be cached across users when behind a CDN
author: sashor
body:
<!--
Please note that but submitting a bug report, you agree to our Code of Conduct: http://code-of-conduct.voxmedia.com/

When submitting a bug report, provide as much detail as possible, including a screenshot or copy-paste of any related error messages, logs, or other output that might be related. Places to look for information include your browser console, server console, and network logs. The more information you can give the better.

Please help us by doing the following steps before logging an issue:
  * Search for other similar issues: https://github.com/coralproject/talk/search
  * Read our docs: https://docs.coralproject.net

Please fill in the *entire* template below.
-->
When Coral is deployed behind a CDN or reverse proxy with shared caching enabled, authenticated GraphQL responses may be cached without varying by the Authorization header.

This can potentially lead to responses being reused across different authenticated users if the caching layer does not differentiate requests based on authentication context.

Additionally, if a public GraphQL request is cached first, a subsequent authenticated request may receive the cached public response depending on CDN configuration.

**Expected behavior:**
GraphQL responses should be explicitly varied based on authentication context to prevent shared caching between:

- Authenticated users

- Public and authenticated requests

Responses should either:

- Include ```Vary: Authorization```

- Or be marked as ```Cache-Control: private```

- Or otherwise prevent shared caching between authenticated users

**Actual behavior:**
When GraphQL cache headers are enabled and Coral is deployed behind a shared caching layer (CDN / reverse proxy), authenticated responses do not explicitly vary by Authorization.

As a result, responses may be stored and reused across users depending on the caching configuration.

For example, fields such as ```viewerReactionPresence``` or other user-specific fields may reflect the state of a previously cached response rather than the current authenticated user.

**Related Issues:** <!-- Did you find other bugs that looked similar? -->
None found.

**Versions:**

- NodeJS: 20.18.1
- NPM: 10.8.2
- MongoDB: 8.0.19
- Redis: N/A
- Browser: N/A
- OS: macOS

title: Coral automatically tracking internal events to our RudderStack instance
author: zgjimgjonbalaj
body:
We've been trying to figure out why we were getting events like `fetch.useLiveNotificationsPollingQuery` and others sent to our RudderStack instance without luck. This was until we accidentally clicked on our Coral comments form and the XHR tab lit up like a Christmas tree.  Turns out that

[https://github.com/coralproject/talk/blob/develop/client/src/core/client/stream/App/listeners/OnEventsForRudderStack.tsx#L20](https://github.com/coralproject/talk/blob/develop/client/src/core/client/stream/App/listeners/OnEventsForRudderStack.tsx#L20)

is sending these internal events via the global window.rudderanalytics without any checks or configuration (that I could find). Seems like this is an easy way to clutter/pollute analytics, is there a way to disable entirely?

**Expected behavior:**
`window.rudderanalytics` is defined by RudderStack JS SDK and cannot be renamed/disabled without breaking the functionality of RudderStack. Additionally, overriding the functions and performing checks is kind of cumbersome and would like to avoid. Coral should offer a way to manually enable `window.rudderanalytics` and thus by default disable entirely.

**Actual behavior:**
Coral sends a myriad of events to our instance for all users.

title: gmail duplicate detection will ban regular gmail addresses with numbers & letters

title: Comment moderator access issue
author: mcahill-cpu
body:

I successfully grant it to him and it works, but then a few hours later he's no longer able to moderate, and when I go into Coral his role has changed back from moderator to commenter. I'd appreciate any help fixing this issue! Thank you!

title: long-lived token
author: sofiadipace
body:
Hi there! 😊

I have a quick question regarding the tokens generated using the `coral-cli` ([Doku](https://docs.coralproject.net/cli#coral-cli-tokencreate))

Our team generated some tokens internally, and we assumed they were long-lived since they didn’t include an `exp` claim. 
When decoding the token, we see the following claims:

```
{
  "pat": true,
  "iat": xxxxx,
  "nbf": xxxxxxx,
  "iss": "xxx",
  "sub": "xxxx",
  "jti": "xxxx"
}
```
They have been valid for a very long time. In the coral docu it stands, that they currently don't expire. However, today we encountered the following error when trying to execute a mutation against Coral:
```
code: "TOKEN_INVALID"
type: "INVALID_REQUEST_ERROR"
```
So our question is: are these tokens truly long-lived, or is there an internal expiration mechanism even without an exp claim?

Thanks in advance for your help!

title: How to log into Coral Admin with SSO?
author: mestru17
body:
We have some SSO users that we have upgraded to moderators, but how do they log into the Coral Admin interface? When they visit Coral Admin, they are presented with the normal email/password login page `/admin/login`, but they don't have a password set up for Coral Admin - they only have an SSO token. Do they need to pass that token in somewhere, or do we need to somehow set up a password for them?

We somehow managed to create a password for one of the users - it is unclear how, but we hit some Coral Admin page, where it prompted the user to create password, and after that they have been able to log in with that.

**Edit:** I should note that we have integrated with Coral via the GraphQL API and not with the embed.
